channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: Mehmet Oz, Donald Trumpâs most effective TV star
item.description: The telegenic doctor enacting the presidentâs agenda with professionalism and zeal

item.title: Europe is balking at cutting welfare to fund defence
item.description: Most Europeans want stronger armed forces, but are unwilling to pay for them

item.title: What Indians are reading, and what that reveals about the self
item.description: English-language books offer a glimpse of Indiaâs hopes and dreams

item.title: Why Britons now trust weather forecasters
item.description: Credit greater accuracy and better communication

item.title: What Europe and NATO must do to be ready for war
item.description: Defence is being re-energised. Now industry must produce more, better and faster, write Ursula von der Leyen and Mark RutteÂ

item.title: Plot Twist: Taylor Swiftâs weddingâyou canât shake it off
item.description: Rachel Lloyd, our deputy culture editor, explores the fascination surrounding the nuptials of the worldâs biggest pop star

item.title: Fear and Doping in Las Vegas
item.description: Inside Enhanced Inc

item.title: Iranâs regime hopes Khameneiâs funeral will demonstrate its strength
item.description: It reveals how much it has already changed

item.title: How little exercise can you get away with?
item.description: Even tiny bursts of activity have measurable benefits

item.title: What to watch this week
item.description: Tales of resilience, from the Founding Fathers to fine dining

item.title: 250 years of America
item.description: Our weekly podcast on America. This week, a health-check on the American system of government

item.title: Inside the fantasy world of Donald Trump
item.description: âRegime Changeâ is an eye-popping report on âthe most powerful man the planet has ever knownâ

item.title: The 250-year experiment: Americaâs birthday
item.description: A special episode of the daily podcast taking a hard look at the country at its semiquincentennial

item.title: Will the data-centre backlash derail the AI boom?
item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, Americaâs planning boards donât believe in moving fast or breaking things

item.title: Who was behind those drone incursions over Europe?
item.description: A new report finds it was probably Russia

item.title: Economic data, commodities and markets
A23a had more adventures than most
item.description: The worldâs biggest iceberg has finally melted away, aged 40 or so

item.title: Heinz tomato ketchup and the sweet taste of market dominance
item.description: The condiment that conquered the world was created 150 years ago

item.title: Amorality and greed at Silicon Valleyâs favourite university
item.description: A student journalist examines the culture at Stanford

item.title: A Danish bestseller about butterflies has set hearts aflutter
item.description: âThe Butterfly Seasonâ is about discovery, mortality and eternity

item.title: Route 66: How a century-old highway helps explain America
item.description: Driving from Chicago to Santa Monica you glimpse the best aspects of the country

item.title: Is The Economist always wrong?
item.description: We used artificial intelligence to test the accuracy of our forecasts

item.title: Indonesia gives its best-known entrepreneur a decade in jail
item.description: The technology giant he built is being yanked around by the state

item.title: The Toddyssey
item.description: You could read Homer. You could see the film. Or you could just go to the office

item.title: NATO ponders how to defend Eastern Europe as America pulls back
item.description: German tanks are returning to a region they once razed

item.title: Brazilians are going gaga for Chinese brands
item.description: They think China has better tech than America

item.title: The Caribbean has a problem with pesticides
item.description: It uses them more intensively than anywhere in the world

item.title: Promised reforms could change Cuba radically
item.description: But only if they actually go ahead

item.title: The next great Middle East rivalry
item.description: Turkey and Israel are natural allies and yet they are increasingly at odds

item.title: Africaâs new middle class is putting down roots in the suburbs
item.description: For a glimpse of the continentâs future, visit the edges of its burgeoning cities

item.title: Ugandaâs volatile army chief goes after the press
item.description: Muhoozi Kainerugaba, the presidentâs son, looks increasingly like a despot-in-waiting

item.title: Donald Trumpâs reflecting-pool debacle is a gift to America
item.description: What a fitting way to honour its birthday

item.title: Africans are turning to Starlink
item.description: They are fed up with the continentâs terrible internet

item.title: Happy birthday America, the ally Europe admiresâquestioningly
item.description: A tale of two continents

item.title: Ukraine is trying to threaten Vladimir Putin into peace talks
item.description: Its claims to be winning the drone war are partly propaganda and partly real

item.title: The ascent of the chippy southerner
item.description: Andy Burnham offers something precious to the south: grievance

item.title: Death and dishonesty in British maternity hospitals
item.description: Yet another report paints a bleak picture

item.title: Chinese carmakers aim to go upmarket in Britain
item.description: But even with slick ads, that end of the market will be tough to crack

item.title: Was the Trump administrationâs blocking of Anthropicâs Fable and Mythos models dystopian?
item.description: Also this week, startups in Honduras, sharing companiesâ wealth, cricket in India, China, reading

item.title: Turkey and Israel should trade energy, not insults
item.description: Both have much to gain from being less belligerent

Cartoon: A toast to Americaâs 250th birthday
item.description: A lighter take on the news

item.title: Politics
Plot Twist newsletter: What to read to celebrate America at 250
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, offers reading recommendations for July 4thÂ Â

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: Why 2008 was a turning point in America
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, explains how the year laid the groundwork for todayâs divisions

item.title: Venezuelaâs earthquakes are partly Americaâs problem
item.description: The governmentâs response has been dire. Its patron has a duty to help

item.title: Venezuelans are furious with the American-backed regime
item.description: But the earthquakes could give it an excuse to keep putting off elections

item.title: Indiaâs government overhauls a vast workfare programme
item.description: Critics say it has been gutted

item.title: Pakistanâs army chief battles with its imprisoned ex-prime minister
item.description: Which of Asim Munir or Imran Khan will prevail?

item.title: Roaring trades: oil majorsâ secret success story
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: Americaâs capricious grip on frontier AI models and the changing business of concert tours

item.title: America should not imprison frontier AI
item.description: Fable is free. But the technology desperately needs better regulations

item.title: Americaâs Wrecking-ball revolution
item.description: The architects of the post-war order are tearing it down. Edward Carr asks what will rise from the rubble

item.title: Andy Burnhamâs devolution must be more than a âManc-a-Lagoâ gimmick
item.description: Britainâs next leader will also have to confront a culture of tolerable decline, writes Mark Sedwill

item.title: Is Germanyâs government finally getting its act together?
item.description: A new set of reforms has lifted sagging spirits in Berlin

item.title: How much will a 4th of July cookout cost this year?
item.description: We calculate the rising price of Americansâ summer staples

item.title: Are stablecoins money?
item.description: Policymakersâ job is to make them safe as well as useful

item.title: Unpacking Venezuelaâs peculiar debt restructuring
item.description: Hazy numbers and the absence of the IMF make for an unusual negotiation

item.title: America is anxious, and awesomely powerful
item.description: Restlessness is what prevents the republic from sinking into stagnation

item.title: Asiaâs elderly are being neglectedÂ
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: We woz wrong about oil
item.description: The market bested us. But for The Economist, there is no shame in that

item.title: Show a liberal a Lime bike and he will show you his soul
item.description: Are the e-bikes a paragon of liberalism or an abomination?

item.title: Can Bending Spoons thrive as a listed company?
item.description: A rare Italian software star uses the private-equity playbook

item.title: Transforming the cradle of the Confederacy
item.description: Tourists are flocking to monuments on Americaâs racial history

item.title: Scientists take another step towards lab-made life
item.description: What do you call an organism with no evolutionary ancestors? SpudCell, apparently

item.title: China hushes up a plane crash in the heart of its capital
item.description: A light aircraft flew into Beijingâs tallest building. A news blackout followed

item.title: Should you get your babyâs genome sequenced?
item.description: Our podcast on science and technology. Our correspondent weighs up the benefits and risks of newborn screening programmes

item.title: Americaâs third century will be shared with China
item.description: Beijingâs great advantage is its scaleâbut a coalition of allies can match it, writes Rush Doshi

item.title: Europeâs airport queues are extremely longâand getting longer
item.description: The continentâs border-control system is out of control

item.title: Scientists can now study the event horizons of black holes
item.description: They make the universeâs most extreme gravitational laboratories

item.title: Why Cambridge and Cambridge need each other
item.description: Two of the worldâs most innovative cities have the same name and completely opposite problems. That is leading to closer collaboration

item.title: America is mightyâbut becoming less dominant
item.description: Ahead of its 250th birthday, an empirical look shows the worldâs leading country no longer stands so far apart

item.title: Fault lines: Venezuelaâs paltry earthquake response
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: the frontier of food-delivery robots, and the mysteriesâand mysteryâof Harlan Coben

item.description: The governmentâs controversial defence investment plan finally breaks cover

item.description: The conditions and will for a deal are not what they were in 1985

item.description: The 30-year-old populist will run if Marine Le Pen is banned. Who is he?

item.description: Recent attacks underscore the limits of diplomacy between Iran and America

item.description: The justices handed the president some notable defeats. But their biggest decisions strengthened him

item.description: As undesirable as an imperial executive is, the court read the constitution correctly, argues Sai Prakash

item.description: The court has over-extended presidential power, writes Victoria Nourse

item.description: Tighter ties with the mainland have not always helped
